bible:
IDENTITY
- Title: Blood and Jade Sky
- Pitch: A forgotten prince of Tikal reclaims his legacy as his stolen invention becomes the ruin of the city he once saved
- Setting: The Maya city-state of Tikal in 800 AD, during the height of its political and architectural rivalry with Calakmul

PROTAGONIST
- Name: K’inich Ahau-T’ot’ a.k.a. T’ot’—called El Cuervo by the court
- Public face: A humble scribe and stonemason, often overlooked despite his intricate glyphs and calendars
- Hidden truth: The last living heir of the Ajaw Kan dynasty of Tikal, hidden in the royal scriptorium after the massacre of his family
- The wound: His older brother, Crown Prince Chan-Bahlum, died saving him during the coup—his death was falsely recorded as K’inich's, making Chan-Bahlum the martyr and K’inich forgotten

ANTAGONIST
- Name: Lord Wak-K’in, High Architect and Speaker of the Council
- Why they feel justified: Believes Tikal’s survival depends on his vision—his monumental calendar system, not K’inich’s—even if it means erasing the past to glorify the present
- What they're protecting or pursuing: The construction of the Great Twin Pyramid complex as a monument to Wak-K’in’s leadership and a tool to consolidate power before the next katun cycle

ENABLER
- Name: Ixchel, Royal Seamstress and Keeper of the Bloodline Tapestry
- Their relationship to the protagonist: Once a sworn handmaiden to the royal children, now secretly shelters K’inich and keeps his identity hidden
- Why they fail the protagonist: In fear of reprisal for harboring a displaced prince, she withholds critical glyph translations that could prove his lineage, stalling his recovery of truth

CATALYST
- Name: Lady Ti-Hu, Ambassador to Calakmul and Spymaster
- Why they're dangerous: A master of psychological warfare, they plant false histories and forge documents to destabilize Tikal from within, all while feeding Wak-K’in misinformation to fuel his arrogance

WORLD
- Institution: The Royal Scriptorium and Calendar Guild of Tikal, a closed order of scribes, astronomers, and architects who control knowledge, time, and memory through stone and bark
- Key locations: Temple of the Giant Jaguar—home of the royal scribes and where K’inich hides; Great Twin Pyramid Complex—where Wak-K’in’s flawed calendar is inscribed into public memory; The Bloodline Obelisk—where royal genealogies are carved, now defaced; The Black Cenote—used by spies and exiles for secret meetings
- What everyone assumes about the power structure: That the ruling council, led by the High Architect, holds true authority—only the scribes secretly know the king’s bloodline matters most in sacred rites

STAKES
- What the protagonist secretly holds: The complete astronomical codex *Tzolk’in Haab’*—a predictive system so accurate it could have prevented the coup if used properly, and which proves K’inich as the rightful heir
- What's being taken from them: His birthright to the throne of Tikal and the credit for the *Tzolk’in Haab’*, now attributed to Wak-K’in as the “Great Timekeeper”
- The tangible proof: The Bloodline Obelisk’s hidden chamber contains carved glyphs depicting K’inich as the surviving prince—hidden by the royal astrologer before the fall
- The ticking clock: The next katun cycle begins in 818 days—Wak-K’in plans to complete the Twin Pyramid by then and erase all doubt through sheer monumentality, making K’inich’s claim impossible to restore

DRAMATIC IRONY
- What the reader knows from chapter one: That T’ot’ the scribe is K’inich in disguise, and that his simple glyphs hide the key to Tikal’s fall
- What the antagonist won't learn until too late: That the system Wak-K’in is building is flawed—its eclipse predictions fail when K’inich secretly corrects them—but by then, the city will already be in chaos

LOVE INTEREST
- Name: Ahau-Batz’, Master Astronomer and Disgraced Heir of Dos Pilas
- Their connection to the conflict: His father was Wak-K’in’s rival, exiled for questioning the calendar’s accuracy—now he works in the scriptorium under Ixchel’s protection
- What keeps them apart: His name is tied to a rival dynasty; K’inich fears entangling him in dynastic revenge
- What will bring them together: A shared night under the stars calculating an impossible eclipse—where they realize love is worth more than bloodlines—igniting a public ritual that forces truth
beats:
**CHAPTER 1: The Mask**
Summary: K’inich Ahau-T’ot’ (posing as T’ot’), a humble scribe in Tikal’s royal scriptorium, meticulously carves a glyph panel depicting the Bloodline Obelisk’s hidden chamber—his hiding place’s future undoing—while scribes dismiss him as talentless.
Emotional temperature: unease
Characters present: T’ot’ (K’inich), Junior Scribe Chak, Scribe Master Itzel
Cliffhanger: A blood-red quill, left carelessly on his workbench, begins to drip ink onto the floor—though no one saw it moved.
Dramatic irony: The reader knows T’ot’s simple glyphs conceal the *Tzolk’in Haab’*’s code, while the scribes see only his “mediocre” work.

---

**CHAPTER 2: The Strike**
Summary: During the Festival of the Maize God, Lord Wak-K’in publicly humiliates T’ot’ by ordering him to recite a flawed eclipse prediction—exposing his ignorance—while the court laughs and K’inich’s fingers tremble with held-back fury.
Emotional temperature: humiliation
Characters present: Lord Wak-K’in, T’ot’, Lady Ti-Hu, Ahau-Batz’, Court Observers
Cliffhanger: Ahau-Batz’’s horrified gasp lingers as he recognizes the error’s origin in the real *Tzolk’in Haab’*—but he says nothing.
Dramatic irony: Wak-K’in believes he’s crushed a rival, unaware the *real* codex exists elsewhere.

---
**CHAPTER 3: The Silence**
Summary: After the festival, T’ot’ locks himself in the scriptorium’s lower vault, sketching a map of the Bloodline Obelisk’s hidden chamber—while Ixchel, watching from the shadows, clutches a shred of the royal tapestry (proof of his lineage) but says nothing.
Emotional temperature: tension
Characters present: T’ot’, Ixchel
Cliffhanger: A shadowy figure (Lady Ti-Hu’s spy) slips a false edict into T’ot’s satchel—an order for his arrest by week’s end.
Dramatic irony: Ixchel knows the tapestry’s secret but fears giving it to him; the reader sees her fear as cowardice, not caution.

---
**CHAPTER 4: The Strut**
Summary: Lord Wak-K’in hosts a grand ceremony in the Great Twin Pyramid’s shadow, unveiling his "perfect" calendar system as the crowd chants his name—while in a private chamber, he admits to his advisor that the eclipse calculations “feel heavy”—as if unseen hands tweaked them.
Emotional temperature: satisfaction
Characters present: Lord Wak-K’in, High Priest Kinich, Advisor Xam
Cliffhanger: Wak-K’in’s advisor fingers a glyph tablet showing K’inich’s name erased—and hesitates before burning it.
Dramatic irony: Wak-K’in takes credit for K’inich’s work, unaware the system is already flawed.

---
**CHAPTER 5: The Overreach**
Summary: Wak-K’in orders Ahau-Batz’ to recalculate the Twin Pyramid’s alignment—only for Ahau-Batz’ to realize the stones are placed incorrectly, causing a structural wobble that sends workers scrambling.
Emotional temperature: unease
Characters present: Wak-K’in, Ahau-Batz’, Junior Architect Chik’in
Cliffhanger: Chik’in whispers, “This is how the old pyramid at Dos Pilas fell…” before Wak-K’in silences him with a glare.
Dramatic irony: Ahau-Batz’ suspects sabotage but doesn’t know it’s inherited knowledge from his exiled father.

---
**CHAPTER 6: The Quiet Move**
Summary: Under cover of night, T’ot’ enters the Black Cenote, exchanging stolen obsidian tools for a hidden bundle from a Calakmul exile—Ahau-Batz’’s father’s star charts—which reveal the Twin Pyramid’s fatal flaw.
Emotional temperature: tension
Characters present: T’ot’, Calakmul Exile (disguised)
Cliffhanger: The exile hisses, “The architect’s arrogance is his tomb”—before vanishing into the mist.
Dramatic irony: Wak-K’in’s men patrol above, oblivious the scriptorium’s “nobody” just secured proof of his downfall.

---
**CHAPTER 7: The Wobble**
Summary: At the next scribal council, Scribe Master Itzel stumbles over a routine glyph translation—then, in a rare slip, murmurs, “The old prince’s name… it’s here…” before correcting himself with a cough.
Emotional temperature: humiliation
Characters present: Itzel, Scribes, T’ot’ (watching from the back)
Cliffhanger: A scribe drops a clay pot, shattering it—and the sound echoes like a drumbeat through the chamber.
Dramatic irony: The scribes fear a curse, but T’ot’ knows it’s the *Tzolk’in Haab’*’s first tremor.

---
**CHAPTER 8: The Bleed**
Summary: Three disasters strike simultaneously: the Twin Pyramid’s central staircase cracks; Lady Ti-Hu’s spy reports “rumors of a hidden heir” in the scriptorium; and Ahau-Batz’ arrives late to work, his hands bandaged (from smashing them against the pyramid’s stones in rage).
Emotional temperature: tension
Characters present: Ahau-Batz’, Scribes, Lady Ti-Hu’s Spy
Cliffhanger: A child in the market shouts, “The scribe who wasn’t there saved us once!”—before being dragged away by Wak-K’in’s guards.
Dramatic irony: Wak-K’in thinks the problems are unrelated; the reader knows they’re converging.

---
**CHAPTER 9: The Offer**
Summary: Wak-K’in corners T’ot’ in the scriptorium’s courtyard, offering a return to favor if he “helps correct the calendar’s errors”—slipping a gold pyrite bead (a royal symbol) into his palm as bait.
Emotional temperature: unease
Characters present: T’ot’, Lord Wak-K’in
Cliffhanger: T’ot’ pockets the bead—but when Wak-K’in leaves, he rolls it into the gutter with his foot.
Dramatic irony: Wak-K’in sees the act as defiance; the reader knows it’s rejection.

---
**CHAPTER 10: The Scope**
Summary: In the scriptorium’s vault, Ahau-Batz’ and T’ot’ compare the *Tzolk’in Haab’*’s hidden pages to the Twin Pyramid’s flawed glyphs—revealing the design’s intentional sabotage—and realize K’inich’s revenge has been decades in the making.
Emotional temperature: triumph
Characters present: T’ot’, Ahau-Batz’
Cliffhanger: A shadow passes the doorway—A spy, but they’re holding a blade.
Dramatic irony: The spies think they’re stealing secrets; the reader knows the secrets were never their target.

---
**CHAPTER 11: The Freefall**
Summary: Lady Ti-Hu’s network collapses: her “loyal” informant in the scriptorium is found dead (poison in his ink), Wak-K’in’s priestly allies refuse to perform a ritual, and Ixchel flees the palace with the Bloodline Tapestry—leaving her hiding place exposed.
Emotional temperature: humiliation
Characters present: Lady Ti-Hu, Ixchel, Wak-K’in’s Guards
Cliffhanger: A guard shouts, “The prince’s name is carved in the obelisk!”—as Wak-K’in’s chamber door bursts open.
Dramatic irony: Wak-K’in still thinks Ti-Hu is his ally; the reader knows she’s fleeing for her life.

---
**CHAPTER 12: The Reveal**
Summary: Wak-K’in corners T’ot’ in the Bloodline Obelisk’s chamber, where the hidden glyphs glow under torchlight—revealing K’inich as the surviving prince, his brother’s false death, and Wak-K’in’s stolen glory.
Emotional temperature: triumph
Characters present: K’inich, Lord Wak-K’in
Cliffhanger: Wak-K’in’s torch slips—and the chamber’s exit collapses in a shower of dust.
Dramatic irony: Wak-K’in thinks this is the end; the reader knows it’s the beginning.

---
**CHAPTER 13: The Mirror**
Summary: Wak-K’in, trapped in the obelisk’s dark, experiences a vision: his own erased name on the obelisk—just as K’inich’s was—and the ghost of Chan-Bahlum whispering, “You stole what wasn’t yours.”
Emotional temperature: tension
Characters present: Wak-K’in (alone), Spectral Chan-Bahlum (imagined)
Cliffhanger: K’inich’s voice echoes from the rubble: “You called me a ghost. Now you *are* one.”
Dramatic irony: Wak-K’in thinks he’s being punished; the reader knows he’s being taught.

---
**CHAPTER 14: The Terms**
Summary: Above ground, K’inich spares Lady Ti-Hu’s life but forces her to reveal Calakmul’s infiltration—while Ahau-Batz’ publicly declares his love in the plaza, risking exile by choosing a rival dynasty’s heir.
Emotional temperature: triumph
Characters present: K’inich, Lady Ti-Hu, Ahau-Batz’, Crowd
Cliffhanger: Wak-K’in, half-buried, roars, “I’ll burn the city before I kneel!”—as the first torch is lit.
Dramatic irony: Wak-K’in still thinks fire is power; the reader knows it’s his funeral pyre.

---
**CHAPTER 15: The Throne**
Summary: K’inich sits on the Ajaw throne, wearing his brother’s jade mask, as Ahau-Batz’ places the *Tzolk’in Haab’* into his hands—and the scribes finally bow not to the calendar, but to the man who wielded it.
Emotional temperature: satisfaction
Characters present: K’inich, Ahau-Batz’, Scribes, Citizens
Cliffhanger: A child drops a jade bead at K’inich’s feet—identical to the one Wak-K’in gave him—now gleaming with royal authority.
Dramatic irony: The cycle is complete; the city finally sees what it missed for decades.
summaries: T’ot’ secretly carved the hidden chamber’s entrance, defying Itzel, then a sentient red quill spelled "Run" in blood-like ink.
T’ot’ exposed Wak-K’in’s flawed eclipse prediction, sparking court outrage, while Ahau-Batz’ revealed his shared secret, uniting against the corrupt system.
T’ot’ secretly maps the Bloodline Obelisk’s hidden chamber, receives proof of his royal lineage from Ixchel, and learns he’s wanted for treason, forcing him to flee to the Black Cenote.
K’inich exposed Wak-K’in’s false eclipse prediction, gained Ahau-Batz’ trust, and was nearly executed when Xam burned his erased name.
Ahau-Batz’ exposed Wak-K’in’s flawed pyramid design, causing it to crack and shift, revealing his deceit and sparking Wak-K’in’s wrath.
T’ot’ and Ahau-Batz’ exchanged stolen charts revealing Wak-K’in’s pyramid lie, evading Lady Ti-Hu’s patrol into the scriptorium’s tunnels.
Itzel nearly revealed K’inich’s true identity, Wak-K’in investigated the disturbance, and Ahau-Batz’ challenged him, while a child’s cryptic words hinted at hidden truths beneath the pyramid.
K’inich and Ahau-Batz’ uncovered a hidden royal glyph, exposing Wak-K’in’s sabotage, while a spy’s discovery and chants revealed K’inich’s secret identity, forcing them to flee from guards.
T’ot’ rejected Wak-K’in’s offer, destroyed the royal bead, and Ahau-Batz’ revealed hidden flaws in the pyramid’s design while an assassin appeared.
T’ot’ discovered Wak-K’in’s sabotage of the calendar, learned he’s the rightful heir K’inich Ahau-T’ot’, and decided to expose Wak-K’in’s lies by correcting the sky, despite the risk of death.

T’ot’ and Ahau-Batz’ fled into the cenote’s depths, Ixchel hid the Bloodline Tapestry revealing Prince K’inich Ahau-T’ot’ alive, and Wak-K’in discovered the obelisk’s disturbance just as the chamber collapsed.
T’ot’ revealed himself as the lost prince K’inich Ahau-T’ot’, shattering Wak-K’in’s power as the ceiling collapsed around them.
K’inich escaped Ti-Hu’s blade, Ahau-Batz’ revealed his alliance, Wak-K’in confronted him, and a child witnessed their conflict, escalating the power struggle.
K’inich uncovered Ti-Hu’s plot to manipulate Wak-K’in’s reign, while Ahau-Batz’ publicly declared loyalty to K’inich, sparking chaos as Wak-K’in set Tikal ablaze.
K’inich exposed Wak-K’in’s deception, revealed the unburned royal bead, and ascended the throne as Ajaw, while Wak-K’in’s power crumbled.
tropeDisplay: disguised-royalty + stolen-credit + let-them-fail + childhood-promise [Maya civilization, Tikal, 800 AD]
